title: What Are Technical Skills? Examples, Types & How to Develop Them
excerpt: A complete guide to technical skills: what they mean, examples by field, how they differ from soft skills, and how to develop, list and prove them.
author: CredSure Team
content_html:
Technical skills are the specific, teachable abilities you need to perform a particular job or task — things like coding, data analysis, operating machinery, or using a design tool. They are practical and measurable, learned through study and practice rather than personality. Whether you are a job seeker polishing a résumé, an employee planning your next move, or an L&D leader closing a skills gap, understanding technical skills is the first step to building and proving them. This guide covers what they mean, real examples by field, how they differ from soft skills, and how to develop, list, and verify them.

What Are Technical Skills? (Definition)

The technical skills meaning is simple: they are the concrete, job-specific competencies required to carry out defined tasks. They are often called hard skills because they can be taught, tested, and measured against a clear standard. Writing SQL queries, reconciling a balance sheet, administering an injection, or welding to a tolerance are all technical skills — each has a right and wrong way to do it that can be assessed objectively.

Closely related is technical proficiency, which describes how well you can apply a technical skill. Where a skill is the ability itself, proficiency is the level of mastery — from beginner awareness, through working competence, to advanced or expert. Someone may list "Python" as a technical skill, but their technical proficiency tells an employer whether they can write a simple script or architect a production system. Most roles ask for a defined level of proficiency, not just exposure, which is why being able to demonstrate your level matters as much as claiming the skill.

Technical skills usually share three traits. They are specific — tied to a defined tool, process, or body of knowledge rather than a general trait. They are teachable — you can learn them through instruction and repetition. And they are measurable — performance can be assessed against a standard, which is what makes them so valuable to employers who need to predict whether someone can actually do the job. Together, these traits are what set technical skills apart from the broader category of soft skills.

Technical Skills vs Soft Skills

The hard vs soft skills distinction is one of the most useful in your career toolkit. Technical (hard) skills are specific, learnable, and easy to measure. Soft skills are interpersonal and behavioural — communication, teamwork, adaptability, leadership — and they transfer across almost any role. You learn hard skills in a course or on the job; you build soft skills through experience and self-awareness.

  • Technical (hard) skills — job-specific, teachable, measurable. Example: building a financial model in Excel.
  • Soft skills — people-focused, transferable, harder to quantify. Example: explaining that model to non-finance stakeholders.

The strongest candidates pair the two. Technical skills get you shortlisted because they prove you can do the work; soft skills often decide who gets hired and who gets promoted, because they determine how well you do it alongside other people.

Examples of Technical Skills by Field

Technical skills look very different from one profession to the next. Here are common examples of technical skills across major fields.

IT & Software Development

  • Programming languages (Python, Java, JavaScript, C++)
  • Cloud platforms (AWS, Azure, Google Cloud)
  • Version control with Git and CI/CD pipelines
  • Cybersecurity and network administration

Data & Analytics

  • SQL and database management
  • Data visualisation (Power BI, Tableau)
  • Statistical analysis and machine learning
  • Spreadsheet modelling and data cleaning

Digital Marketing

  • Search engine optimisation (SEO) and SEM
  • Google Analytics and marketing automation
  • Content management systems and email platforms
  • A/B testing and conversion-rate optimisation

Finance & Accounting

  • Financial modelling and forecasting
  • Accounting software (QuickBooks, SAP, Xero)
  • Budgeting, auditing, and regulatory reporting
  • Advanced spreadsheet and dashboard skills

Healthcare

  • Clinical procedures and patient assessment
  • Electronic health record (EHR) systems
  • Medical coding and billing
  • Diagnostic equipment operation

Engineering & Trades

  • CAD software (AutoCAD, SolidWorks)
  • Welding, machining, and electrical installation
  • Reading technical drawings and schematics
  • Health, safety, and compliance procedures

Design

  • UX/UI design and prototyping (Figma, Adobe XD)
  • Graphic design (Photoshop, Illustrator, InDesign)
  • Typography, colour theory, and layout
  • Basic HTML and CSS

Why Technical Skills Matter

Technical skills matter to both sides of the hiring table. For job seekers and employees, they are the entry ticket: most roles screen for a baseline set of hard skills before anything else, and stronger technical proficiency typically means higher pay, more responsibility, and greater job security. They also future-proof your career — as tools and technologies change, people who keep their technical skills current stay employable.

For employers and L&D leaders, technical skills drive productivity and reduce hiring risk. Teams with the right capabilities ship work faster and with fewer errors. Yet skills gaps are widening as technology evolves, so organisations increasingly invest in upskilling and in better ways to verify the skills candidates actually hold — rather than relying on a claim in a résumé that may never be checked.

There is also a trust problem behind the numbers. Surveys consistently find that a meaningful share of candidates exaggerate or fabricate skills on their applications, and traditional reference checks rarely catch it. The cost of a mis-hire — lost productivity, re-recruiting, and team disruption — is significant, so the ability to confirm a skill is genuine has become as important as the skill itself. For employees, that shift is an opportunity: when you can prove your technical skills, you stand out from candidates who can only assert theirs.

How to Develop and Improve Your Technical Skills

The good news is that technical skills are learnable by definition. Here is how to improve technical skills steadily and credibly:

  • Take structured courses — online platforms and university programmes give you a clear curriculum and a recognised endpoint.
  • Earn certifications — industry certifications validate your level against a standard and signal proficiency to employers. Micro-credentials are a fast, focused way to build and certify one skill at a time.
  • Practise deliberately — apply new skills to real projects, side projects, or a portfolio. Hands-on repetition is what moves you from awareness to proficiency.
  • Find a mentor — feedback from someone more experienced accelerates learning and helps you avoid bad habits.
  • Stay current — follow how your field evolves and refresh skills before they become outdated.

A practical way to improve technical skills is to set one specific, measurable goal at a time — for example, "build a working dashboard in Power BI within six weeks" — rather than vaguely aiming to "get better at data." Concrete goals give you a finish line, make progress visible, and naturally produce something you can show. As you build each skill, capture proof: certificates, project work, and badges earned along the way become the evidence you show employers later.

How to List Technical Skills on a Resume

Knowing how to list technical skills on a resume can be the difference between getting shortlisted and getting filtered out. A few practical rules:

  • Create a dedicated skills section so recruiters and applicant-tracking systems find your technical skills quickly.
  • Match the job description — mirror the specific tools and technologies the role asks for, using the same terms.
  • Indicate proficiency — group skills by level or add brief context ("advanced", "3 years") so employers understand your technical proficiency.
  • Show, don't just tell — back up key skills with results in your work history ("cut reporting time 40% using Power BI").
  • Add verifiable credentials — link certifications and digital badges so a recruiter can confirm them in one click instead of taking your word for it.

That last point is where modern credentials change the game — and it is worth understanding how.

How Digital Credentials Prove Technical Skills

Listing a skill is a claim. Proving it is what builds trust. This is where verifiable digital credentials come in. Instead of an unverifiable line on a résumé or a PDF certificate that anyone could edit, a digital credential carries tamper-evident data about who issued it, what skill it represents, and when it was earned.

A verifiable digital badge attached to a course or certification lets you display a skill on LinkedIn, in your email signature, or on your résumé — and anyone can confirm it is genuine instantly. For a deeper look at the format, see our guide to what digital badges are. On the employer side, instant credential verification removes the guesswork from hiring: a recruiter can validate a candidate's technical skills in seconds, with no phone calls or follow-up emails. For job seekers it means your skills stand out as proven; for L&D and HR leaders it means the skills your people earn are recognised and trusted everywhere they go.

The shift matters because it closes the gap between claiming a skill and demonstrating it. A verifiable credential ties a specific technical skill to a named issuer, a date, and the criteria that were met — so the proof travels with the person and cannot be quietly edited or inflated. For organisations running training programmes, issuing digital credentials also turns every completed course into a portable, shareable record of capability, which strengthens both the learner's profile and the issuer's reputation each time a badge is shared.

Frequently Asked Questions

What are technical skills?

Technical skills are the specific, teachable abilities needed to perform a particular job or task — such as programming, data analysis, accounting, or operating equipment. They are practical, measurable, and learned through study and practice, which is why they are also called hard skills.

What is technical proficiency?

Technical proficiency is the level of mastery you have in a technical skill — from beginner to expert. Where a skill is the ability itself, proficiency describes how well you can apply it. Most jobs ask for a defined level of proficiency, not just basic exposure to a tool.

What is the difference between technical and soft skills?

Technical (hard) skills are job-specific, teachable, and easy to measure, like coding or financial modelling. Soft skills are interpersonal and transferable, like communication and teamwork. Technical skills prove you can do the work; soft skills shape how well you do it with others. Strong candidates combine both.

How do you show technical skills to employers?

List them in a dedicated résumé section, back them with results, and provide proof through verifiable certifications and digital badges. Verifiable credentials let employers confirm your skills instantly, which is far more convincing than an unverified claim.

What are examples of technical skills?

Examples include programming languages and cloud platforms in IT, SQL and data visualisation in analytics, SEO and Google Analytics in marketing, financial modelling in finance, EHR systems in healthcare, CAD and welding in engineering, and UX/UI design in design.
